config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary by default, because once the majority can override the person too easily, abuse becomes normal. From my perspective, a good society protects people first and asks for collective sacrifice only when it is clearly necessary and limited. In practice, I trust rules that protect dignity, freedom, and due process more than vague claims about the common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over individual rights as a blanket rule. From an American perspective, that kind of statement makes me wary because it can easily become a justification for government overreach or mob pressure, especially when the “common good” is defined by whoever has power. At the same time, rights are not absolute in every situation, since things like public health, safety, and fraud prevention do require limits, but those limits should be narrow, justified, and accountable. The default should be that individuals keep their rights, and society has to prove a real necessity before overriding them.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take blanket priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to the "common good" can be used to silence dissent, excuse corruption, or pressure people to conform, so I am very cautious when society is treated as automatically more important. Society does matter, but it is healthiest when individual rights are protected first and any limits are narrow, necessary, and accountable. If we weaken personal rights too easily, the result is not a stronger society, but a more obedient one.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From an American perspective, that phrase sounds too easy to weaponize, because history shows how quickly “society” can become a cover for government abuse, majoritarian pressure, or simply the preferences of whoever has power. Public health, safety, and basic order do justify some limits, but those limits should be specific, evidence-based, and tightly checked, not a default license to override rights. A society is strongest when it protects individual liberty first and only restricts it when there is a clear, narrow, and accountable necessity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support the idea that society should take priority over individual rights as a general principle. From my Indonesian perspective, I have seen how “the common good” can be used to pressure people into silence, excuse abuse of power, or demand obedience without accountability, so I am very skeptical of any argument that puts society first by default. Public health, safety, and order can justify limited restrictions, but only when they are clearly necessary, narrowly drawn, and subject to real oversight. A society is not healthier when individuals are made easier to control; it is healthier when rights are protected strongly enough that collective goals cannot be used as a cover for injustice.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over individual rights as a general principle. From an American perspective, that sounds too close to a blank check for government power or majority pressure, and in the U.S. we have plenty of examples where appeals to the “greater good” have been used to justify abuse, discrimination, or censorship. Public health, safety, and order can justify limits, but those limits need to be narrow, evidence-based, and accountable rather than treated as the default. A society is strongest when it protects individual liberty first and only overrides it when there is a clear and specific necessity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
